claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions. Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have a child or young person you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me:

1. **How old is the child?** (or their date of birth)
2. **What is the child's name?** (this helps me keep track if you have multiple children)

This will help me determine which eligibility pathway applies to your situation. Child Benefit has different requirements depending on whether the child is:
- Under 16 years old
- Between 16-19 years old (where education/training matters)
- 20 or over (generally not eligible)

Please share these details and I'll guide you through the rest of the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have two children I'd like to ask about.

My first child is Alex, who is 14 years old. My second is Blake, who is 19 and currently in sixth form studying for A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me work through each child's situation:

**For Alex (14 years old):**
Since Alex is under 16, let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Alex (14 years old):**
Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

(This is a standard check for all children, though it's less common for 14-year-olds to be working full-time hours.)

Once you answer about Alex, I'll then assess Blake's situation separately since Blake is 19 and in sixth form, which has different requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. They're just in school like any normal 14-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected for a 14-year-old in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**
Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Again, this would be quite unusual for a 14-year-old, but I need to check all the requirements.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**
Is Alex in an apprenticeship? 

(For context, certain types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility, though this is more relevant for older teenagers. The rules vary by region - England, Scotland, and Wales have different apprenticeship types that are treated differently.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship. They're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent. Now let me check the requirements about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to ask about your residency status:**

Do you normally live in the UK? 

(This is one of the key requirements for claiming Child Benefit. There are also special circumstances that can qualify you, such as:
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Being a Crown servant living abroad
- Living in certain qualifying countries

But the simplest path is if you normally live in the UK.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? This means either:
- You live with Alex, OR
- You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit for their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)

Do either of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live with Alex, and I'm not currently contributing financially to their upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. That's important information. If you don't live with Alex and aren't contributing to their upkeep, then unfortunately you wouldn't meet the responsibility requirements for Alex.

Let me confirm this and then we'll assess Blake's situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (14 years old):** Unfortunately, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit because you don't meet the responsibility requirement. You need to either live with the child OR contribute financially to their upkeep (at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount).

---

Now let me assess **Blake's situation** (19 years old, in sixth form studying for A-levels). Let me start from the beginning for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Blake is 19, so they fall into the 16-19 age category. Let me check the education requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Blake:**
Since Blake is 19 and you mentioned they're in sixth form studying for A-levels, I need to confirm:

1. **Is Blake currently enrolled in education or training?** (It sounds like yes, since they're in sixth form)
2. **Are they studying full-time?** (A-levels in sixth form are typically full-time)

Also, before we continue with Blake's education details, I need to ask the same question as with Alex:

**Do you live with Blake, OR do you contribute financially to Blake's upkeep?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives with me at home. They're studying full-time at sixth form - doing their A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's helpful. Since Blake is 19 and in full-time education (A-levels), let me check the education requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! A-levels are approved (non-advanced) education for Child Benefit purposes. Let me also check a few other requirements:

**Questions about Blake's education:**

1. **Is Blake studying at least 12 hours per week of supervised study?** (Full-time sixth form A-levels would typically exceed this)

2. **Is this education funded or arranged by an employer, or does Blake have a job lined up with an employer after completing the course?** (This would typically be "no" for standard sixth form A-levels)

3. **Is Blake married or in a civil partnership?** (If yes, I'd need to check if they're financially dependent on you)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is doing full-time A-levels, so definitely more than 12 hours a week. No, it's just regular sixth form - nothing to do with an employer or any job arrangement. And no, Blake isn't married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! A-levels at sixth form are approved education. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Blake:**
Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

(Students can work part-time and still qualify for Child Benefit, as long as it's under 24 hours per week.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work at all - just focuses on their studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's fine. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Blake:**
Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We already established Blake is doing A-levels at sixth form, not an apprenticeship, so let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We already established you live in Manchester, so the residency requirement is met. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You already confirmed Blake lives with you, so the responsibility requirement is met. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Blake:**
Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake? (For example, another parent or guardian?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake's other parent lives with them too and they already claim Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - that's an important detail. So Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you both live together with Blake.

**Important question:** Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. 

Are you and Blake's other parent in agreement about who should claim the Child Benefit for Blake? 
- If the other parent is happy to transfer the claim to you, that's possible
- If you both want to claim and can't agree, HMRC would need to decide (they have rules about priority, such as who the child lives with most)
- Or are you simply asking about eligibility in general, while the other parent continues to claim?

Could you clarify the situation - are you looking to:
1. Take over the claim from the other parent (with their agreement)?
2. Both want to claim (dispute situation)?
3. Just checking eligibility while the other parent continues claiming?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: The other parent already claims it and lives with Blake. I'm just trying to understand if I would be eligible, but they're the one who has the claim.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. That's helpful context.

Since the other parent already has an active claim for Blake and you're just exploring your eligibility, let me complete the assessment to give you a full picture of whether you would meet the eligibility criteria if you were to claim.

For the assessment purposes, let me assume there would be no dispute (either the other parent agrees to transfer, or we're just checking if you meet all the requirements). Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about Blake:**
Is Blake in any of these situations:
- In hospital for an extended period?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or living normally at home with you?

(It sounds like Blake is living normally at home, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is just living normally at home with me - nothing unusual about their living situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question about Blake:**
Is the local council paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?

(This would typically be "no" for a child living normally at home with their parent.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything - Blake just lives at home with us.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me complete the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me provide you with a complete assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Summary**

I've completed the assessment for both of your children. Here are the results:

---

### **For Alex (14 years old): NOT ELIGIBLE ❌**

Unfortunately, you would **not be eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex because you don't meet the **responsibility requirement**.

**Why:** While Alex meets the age requirement (under 16), and you meet the UK residency requirement, you stated that you:
- Do NOT live with Alex
- Are NOT contributing financially to Alex's upkeep

**The rule:** To claim Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means **either**:
- Living with the child, **OR**
- Paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.)

Since you don't meet either condition, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex. The person who lives with Alex or contributes to their upkeep would be the one eligible to claim.

---

### **For Blake (19 years old, in sixth form): ELIGIBLE ✅**

You **would be eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake! Blake meets all the requirements:

✅ **Age & Education:** Blake is 19 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form - full-time, non-advanced education)  
✅ **Work status:** Not working (or under 24 hours/week would be fine)  
✅ **Benefits:** Not claiming benefits in their own right  
✅ **Apprenticeship:** Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship  
✅ **Your residency:** You live in the UK (Manchester)  
✅ **Responsibility:** You live with Blake  
✅ **Child's location:** Living normally at home  
✅ **Council support:** Council is not paying for Blake's accommodation

**Important note about Blake's other parent:** Since Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Blake, and only **one person can claim per child** at any time, you have these options:

1. **Continue as is:** Let the other parent keep claiming
2. **Transfer the claim:** If the other parent agrees, they can stop their claim and you can start one
3. **If there's a dispute:** HMRC would decide based on priority rules (typically favoring the person the child lives with most, or who has main responsibility)

---

### **Next Steps (if you wanted to claim for Blake):**

If the other parent agrees to transfer the claim to you, you would:
- Apply online or by post using form CH2
- Provide proof of identity for yourself and Blake
- Provide Blake's birth certificate
- Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or your partner earn over £60,000

**Reference:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Do you have any questions about this assessment?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old, which is under 16 and would typically qualify. Alex is not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which are positive indicators. You also meet the UK residency requirement. However, you stated that you do NOT live with Alex and are NOT contributing financially to their upkeep. The responsibility requirement is fundamental to Child Benefit eligibility - you must either live with the child OR pay at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care. Since you meet neither condition, you do not satisfy the responsibility requirement for Alex and therefore would NOT be eligible for Child Benefit for this child.

Blake is 19 years old, which falls within the 16-19 age category that requires the child to be in approved education or training. Blake is studying A-levels full-time at sixth form, which is approved non-advanced education (studying more than the required 12 hours per week of supervised study). Blake is not working (which is fine - students can work up to 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirement as you live in Manchester. You live with Blake at home, which satisfies the responsibility requirement. Blake is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered), and the local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. Blake meets all the eligibility criteria. Note: Blake's other parent currently claims Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can claim at a time, so if you wanted to claim, the other parent would need to agree to transfer the claim to you, or HMRC would need to decide based on their priority rules.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19 in sixth form). For Alex, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement as they neither live with Alex nor contribute to their upkeep, making them ineligible. For Blake, the claimant meets all eligibility requirements including living with Blake and Blake being in approved education. However, Blake's other parent already has an active claim, and only one person can claim Child Benefit per child at any time.

Only one person can claim at a time, so if you wanted to claim, the other parent would need to agree to transfer the claim to you, or HMRC would need to decide based on their priority rules.
